title: Patterns and Repetition
primer: A single fragment shader runs simultaneously on millions of pixels. This makes it ideal for infinite procedural patterns — no texture memory, no bandwidth, just math. The key operation is fract(x), which returns the fractional part of x and effectively tiles the [0,1] range across all of space. Combined with mod for offset tiling and polar coordinates for radial symmetry, you can fill the screen with checkerboards, bricks, hexagons, and beyond.

challenges.title: Checkerboard
challenges.description: Create a black-and-white checkerboard pattern with 8×8 tiles. Use fract() to tile UV space, then determine which tile each pixel is in using floor(), and XOR the parity of the tile indices.
hints: `floor(tiled)` gives integer tile indices. Adding tile.x and tile.y gives an integer whose parity (even vs. odd) alternates diagonally — exactly the checkerboard pattern.
`mod(tile.x + tile.y, 2.0)` returns 0.0 for even sums and 1.0 for odd sums. If you want white on even tiles, use `1.0 - mod(...)` instead.
challenges.unlocks_theory: `floor(x)` strips the fractional part of a number, giving the integer tile index. `mod(n, 2.0)` extracts the parity (0 or 1) of any integer. Together they form a pixel-perfect checkerboard with no texture lookup — the pattern is computed entirely analytically and is infinitely sharp at any resolution or zoom level.

challenges.title: Brick pattern
challenges.description: Build a brick wall pattern: alternate rows are offset by half a brick width. Use fract() for tiling, but offset the U coordinate on odd rows by 0.5 before computing the fractional tile position.
hints: Use `mod(row, 2.0)` to get 0 for even rows and 1 for odd rows. Multiply by 0.5 and add to `tiled.x` before calling `fract`.

The offset must be applied *before* `fract()` so that odd rows start their tile at the halfway point. After `fract`, the local tile space is [0,1] — use `step` with the mortar margin to punch out the gaps.
challenges.unlocks_theory: The key insight in the brick pattern is that `fract` operates on the already-modified coordinate. By adding the row offset *before* `fract`, you effectively shift where each row starts its tile cycle — no conditionals, no texture. This pattern generalizes: any per-tile modification applied before `fract` can produce staggered, rotated, or scaled tiles in each row or column.

challenges.title: Animated tile pulse
challenges.description: Draw a grid of 5×5 circles that pulse in size over time, with each circle delayed by a phase based on its tile index. Combine fract for tiling, floor for tile index, and sin(u_time + phase) for animation.
challenges.hint: After `fract`, the local tile space is [0,1]. Subtracting 0.5 recenters it at the origin so `length(local)` measures distance from the tile center.
hints: After `fract`, local tile space is [0,1]. Subtracting 0.5 recenters each tile at its own origin. Then `length(local)` is the distance from the tile center — perfect for a circle.
Use `tile.x + tile.y` as the phase offset so diagonally adjacent tiles are in phase, creating a wave-like ripple. You can also try `(tile.x * 1.3 + tile.y * 0.7)` for a less symmetric ripple.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Per-tile state (like phase or color) is derived from the tile index via `floor`, while per-pixel geometry within the tile comes from `fract`. This clean separation — tile identity from `floor`, local position from `fract` — is the universal framework for any grid-based procedural effect. Each tile can have unique properties without branching, because the tile index is just another continuous input to your math.

challenges.title: Hexagonal grid
challenges.description: Construct a hexagonal tiling. Hex grids require two staggered rectangular grids; pick the nearest hex center by comparing distances in both grids. Color each hex cell based on its grid index for a rainbow hex mosaic.
hints: The `hexGrid` function already does the hard work — it returns the integer index of the nearest hex cell center. Just call it: `vec2 id = hexGrid(p);`.
Use the returned `id` (a pair of integers) to derive a color. The cosine palette trick `0.5 + 0.5 * cos(...)` maps any float to [0,1] colors without clamping.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Hexagonal grids are trickier than square grids because hex cells do not align to a simple Cartesian lattice. The standard technique uses two interleaved rectangular grids and picks the nearest center with a distance comparison — no modular arithmetic needed. The returned cell index is an ordinary 2D integer coordinate you can hash into any per-cell value: color, rotation, content, or procedural detail.
